title: Chapter Twenty-Three: Ora and William
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D13+D4 | Flashback: England, 1867 -->

The peat smoke has settled into everything — the curtains, the bedclothes, my hair. I breathe it in and it tastes like soot and home, which is a strange thing to feel about a house that isn't mine in a life that isn't mine either. But Ashworth Hall has become mine in the way places become yours when you love someone who belongs to them. Through accumulation. Through proximity. Through the slow accrual of mornings waking in the same bed, the same fire in the hearth, the same crack in the plaster ceiling that I have memorized the way one memorizes a face.

There are seven cracks. I count them sometimes when William is asleep and I cannot close my eyes. The longest runs from the cornice to the medallion around the chandelier hook, a river delta of fractures branching outward like something growing. Like something alive. I count the cracks and I count my breaths and I count the hours until dawn, and this is how I stay myself.

Outside, the rain hammers the windows with the particular violence of an English autumn refusing to end. The ivy on the south wall presses its dark fingers against the glass, and every gust of wind makes the panes shudder in their lead frames. Inside, every hearth in Ashworth Hall is lit. William insists on it. The stone walls hold the cold like a grudge, and he says he won't have me shivering in his house, as though I am something fragile. As though I am something that could break.
I am something that could break. Just not in the way he thinks.
"Stay still," he says. "You've moved."
"I'm breathing, William."
"Can't you do it more decorously?"

I laugh — I can't help it — and his charcoal hovers over the sketchbook, frozen in that patient way of his, his head tilted just slightly, studying me with the focus of a man who has been studying me for months and has not yet tired of the subject. He will never tire of the subject. This is what frightens me.

He's sitting in the chair by the fire, the one with the worn armrests and the horsehair stuffing coming through one seam. The firelight falls across his sketchbook in a gold-orange wash, and I can see the page from where I lie on the bed — my face emerging from the graphite, half-finished, the lines sure and swift because he has drawn this face so many times that his hand knows it better than his own. The curtain is drawn. It is always drawn when he draws me, because the world outside this room cannot know what happens inside it, and so we have learned to love in enclosed spaces, in firelight instead of daylight, in the privacy of a Gothic manor on the moor where no one asks why a visiting relation has stayed so long.
"You're beautiful," he says, not looking up. He says it the way other men state the weather. A fact. Unremarkable in its consistency.
"And you're biased."
"I'm accurate. There's a difference."

The charred taste of peat clings to the back of my throat. I swallow it down. I have grown used to the way this house flavors everything — the tea, the air, the skin at the base of William's throat when I press my lips there. Everything tastes faintly of burning, and I have decided this is romantic rather than oppressive, because I am in love, and love makes arsonists of us all.

He sets the sketchbook aside and crosses to the bed. The mattress dips under his weight and I go to him without thinking, which is the only way I ever go to him — without thinking, without calculating, without the thousand small defenses I have built over centuries. He kisses my forehead, then my temple, then the corner of my jaw, and I close my eyes and let him because there is no version of this story where I don't.
"I want to show you something," he says.

He reaches for the leather satchel beside the bed — the one he keeps his old sketchbooks in, the ones from childhood and school and the years before we met. He pulls out a book smaller than the others, its cover softened by years of handling, the leather cracking at the spine. A child's sketchbook. He opens it and holds it so the firelight catches the page.
A face looks back at me.
My face.

"Weeks before my tenth birthday," William says. His voice is quiet, careful, the voice he uses for things that frighten him. "I'd been dreaming of a girl. Every night. The same girl, the same face, the same eyes. I couldn't get it out of my head. So I drew it."

The sketch is clumsy — a child's hand, a child's lines — but the face is unmistakably, impossibly mine. The wide-set eyes. The particular curve of the jaw. The way the hair falls across the brow, slightly to the left, the way mine does when I haven't pinned it. I have never seen this sketchbook before. I have never shown William how I wear my hair. And yet.
"I've been drawing you since I was a boy," he says. "Before I ever met you. The same face. The same eyes."

He turns more pages. There are dozens. At ten, at twelve, at fifteen — the technique improving, the face sharpening, but always the same face. Always mine. A girl who didn't exist yet, rendered over and over by a boy who had never seen her, with the devotion of someone praying to a saint he wasn't sure was real.

"I thought I was mad," William says. He closes the book. His eyes meet mine, and in them I see something I have no name for — wonder and terror and recognition all braided together, a rope long enough to hang us both. "Then I met you and realized I was right."
My blood runs cold.

The dreams. The drawings. The way his soul has always been tethered to mine — not across years but across centuries, across lives, across the terrible unbroken thread of a love that refuses to stay dead. He has been drawing Orlando. Not Ora. Orlando. The face I have worn in other bodies, in other times, in other configurations of bone and skin. He is drawing the soul through the skin, and he doesn't know it, and I should tell him, and I—
I don't.
"William," I say, and my voice is steady because I have had centuries of practice at steadiness. "That's the most romantic thing anyone has ever said to me."

He smiles, and the sadness in his eyes recedes, and I hate myself for the lie. But what would I say? That I am older than his grandfather's grandfather? That I have worn this face before and will wear it again in forms he cannot imagine? That the reason he dreamed of me is because we have done this dance in other centuries, in other countries, and every time I love him it ends in a choice between my nature and his heart?
I kiss him instead. This is my greatest talent and my most cowardly act — kissing men when I should be telling them the truth.

The rain intensifies. The fire crackles. William's hands find the buttons at the back of my dress and I feel each one give way like a small surrender. The peat smoke curls between us — we are breathing it, exchanging it, passing the same charred air back and forth like a secret. His fingers trace the line of my spine and I arch into him, and the hum starts.

It always starts. I have never told anyone about the hum — the low vibration that lives at the base of my skull, the frequency my body produces when it is too close to love, too saturated with it. My cells are listening. My bones are paying attention. The body I wear is a borrowed costume held together by will and timing, and love is the thing that unstitches it.

I keep the hum at bay. I push it down, down, down into the dark place where I keep all the things that could ruin this. William touches my hip and I feel the hum pulse. He says my name — "Ora" — and the hum sharpens. I press my mouth to his throat and taste soot and salt and the particular warmth of his skin, and the hum is a roar now, a flood behind a dam, and I will not let it through.
"I love you," he whispers into my hair, and the dam strains.
"I love you," I say back, and the dam holds.

The rain hammers. The fire burns. We move together in the great bed with the curtains drawn and seven cracks in the ceiling, and I am careful — so careful — to love him only as much as the architecture of my deception allows. Not too deep. Not too fierce. Not enough to trigger what I can feel building inside me like water rising in a closed room. I modulate my heart like an instrument. I ration my passion like a woman in a siege. And when it's over, when he is holding me and the rain has slowed and the fire has burned down to embers, I think: *one more night. One more night without the shift.*

He falls asleep first. He always does. I lie in the dark and listen to his breathing and count the things I will lose when this ends: the peat smoke. The rain on glass. The way he says my name in the space between sleeping and waking, as though checking that I'm still there. The way he draws me, again and again, as though making sure I'm real.
I am real. I am so real it is going to destroy us both.
It begins at two in the morning. I know this because the clock on the mantle strikes twice, and I am still awake, and William is still asleep, and my skin has started to shimmer.

Not visibly. Not yet. But I can feel it — every cell in my body vibrating at a frequency just below visibility, like a tuning fork struck against the spine of the universe. The hum is no longer a hum. It is a resonance. It is my entire skeleton singing a song I have heard before, the song the body sings when it is preparing to shed its shape and become something else.
*No,* I think. *Not yet. Not now. Not here.*

The bone structure of my face is trying to change. I can feel the jaw widening, the zygomatic arch shifting, the subtle geometry of my skull rearranging itself as though my bones remember a different configuration. My hips. The pressure there — the pelvis beginning its slow rotation, the hip angles narrowing, the flesh at my waist thickening. This is the shift. This is what I do when the century turns or the heart breaks or the love exceeds the container of the body I wear.

The century has not turned. But the emotional rupture is happening anyway. Too much love. Too much intensity. Too many nights of holding the hum at bay until the bay itself collapsed under the weight of what I was holding back. The body is responding to the heart's overload, and the heart is responding to the boy asleep beside me who has been drawing my face since before he knew I existed, and I can feel it all coming apart —

I grip the sheets. I grip them so hard my knuckles crack, and I force myself still. I force every cell back into alignment. I imagine my bones locking, my joints sealing, my skin adhering to the shape it currently holds through nothing but the brute force of my refusal to let it go. It's like holding back a tide. It's like swallowing an ocean. Every breath is a negotiation with a body that wants to become something else, and every exhale is a small victory in a war I cannot win forever.

Four hours. Four hours of lying rigid in the dark while William sleeps beside me, his hand on my hip, his breath warm and even and utterly unaware. Four hours of my jaw trying to widen and my jaw refusing. Four hours of my hips trying to narrow and my hips refusing. Four hours of the hum pressing outward from the inside, pressing against the membrane of my skin, pressing pressing pressing, and me pressing back, and neither of us winning, and both of us exhausted.
By four in the morning I am sweating. By five I am trembling. By dawn I am hollow — emptied out by the effort of remaining myself, the victory so costly it doesn't feel like one.

The shift didn't complete. But it will. The next time we love too deeply — or when the century turns, or when the grief of holding it back finally eclipses the love itself — it will happen. The body only waits. It is patient in a way the heart is not.

Morning, grey and damp. I stand at the window and the glass is cold against my forehead. The moorland stretches to the horizon, brown and endless, the heather long dead and the sky the color of old pewter. I can see the stone wall at the property line, the copse of bare oaks, the road that leads away from Ashworth Hall and back to the world where I am not Orlando, where I am only Ora, where I am only what I appear to be.

Behind me, the sheets rustle. William wakes slowly, the way he always does — surfacing from sleep in stages, like a diver ascending from deep water. I hear him sit up. I hear him find me with his eyes.
"Come back to bed," he says.
"In a moment."

He doesn't wait. He never does. He crosses the room in his shirtsleeves and bare feet, and I feel him before I see him — his arms wrapping around me from behind, his chin resting on my shoulder, his chest warm against my back. I can feel his heartbeat through my dress. It's steady. It's untroubled. It is the heartbeat of a man who does not know that the woman in his arms spent the night preventing her skeleton from rearranging itself.

I am still Ora. The shift didn't take. The jaw is the same. The hips are the same. The face he's been drawing since childhood looks back at him from every mirror, unchanged. But beneath the skin, the hum is still there — quiet now, patient, inevitable. Waiting. The way the moorland waits for rain. The way the fire waits for oxygen. The way the century waits to turn.
"Marry me," he says.

He says it simply. No preamble, no kneeling, no ring produced from a pocket with trembling fingers. Just those two words pressed into the space between my shoulder blade and my neck, spoken against my skin like a second pulse.
I stare at the moorland. The grey sky. The road that leads away.
I said nothing.

I didn't say yes. I didn't say no. I stood at the window of Ashworth Hall with William's arms around me and the hum waiting under my skin, and I said nothing, because saying yes means marriage means proximity means the shift will happen and he'll see what I become, and saying no means the grief might trigger the shift anyway, and there is no safe answer, there has never been a safe answer, there is only the hum and the love and the impossible arithmetic of being a body that cannot stay and a heart that cannot leave.

His arms tighten. He takes my silence for surprise, or hesitation, or the modesty of a woman who does not expect such an offer. He has no idea that my silence is a door I am locking from the inside, a door that leads to a room where I am Orlando and Ora and everyone in between, and he cannot follow me there without watching me change into something he will not recognize.
"I'll ask again tomorrow," he says, and kisses my shoulder.
The hum stirs. Patient. Inevitable.
The rain begins again.
